Vaiśeṣika Darśana

# Vaiśeṣika Darśana

### A Detailed Overview of Its Ontology, Method, Paramāṇuvāda, and Path to Liberation

[](https://substack.com/@jamvasu)

![Srinivas Jammalamadaka's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!r4yD!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8e86879b-57b7-418c-8baf-81252d34c6c4_96x96.png)

[Srinivas Jammalamadaka](https://substack.com/@jamvasu)

Jun 11, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!5gj5!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd1354b30-7117-4181-888c-16d48556d79e_2752x1536.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!5gj5!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd1354b30-7117-4181-888c-16d48556d79e_2752x1536.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Vaiśeṣika Darśana is one of the foundational systems of Indian philosophical thought. Traditionally attributed to the sage Kaṇāda, also known as Kaṇabhakṣa or Ulūka in later traditions, it is among the most systematic attempts in Bhāratīya thought to understand reality through precise classification, definition, and analysis. While it is often remembered for its Paramāṇuvāda, Vaiśeṣika is not merely a theory of matter. It is a complete darśana: a way of “seeing” reality as it is, so that the human being may move from confusion, bondage, and suffering towards tattva-jñāna, right knowledge of reality, and ultimately niḥśreyasa or mokṣa.

The word darśana comes from the Sanskrit root dṛś, “to see.” In this sense, a darśana is not merely a speculative philosophy, but a disciplined mode of seeing the true nature of things. Vaiśeṣika applies this discipline to the entire field of knowable reality. It asks: What exists? How are things differentiated? What are the irreducible categories through which reality can be understood? What is the relation between matter, motion, mind, self, causality, karma, and liberation? Its answer is given through the doctrine of padārtha, the knowable and nameable categories of reality.

## The Aim of Vaiśeṣika: From Duḥkha to Niḥśreyasa

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Like other Indian darśanas, Vaiśeṣika begins from the existential problem of duḥkha, suffering. Human life is marked by bodily, mental, natural, social, and unseen causes of distress. The philosophical task is not simply to produce abstract theories, but to identify the means by which suffering may be overcome.

The opening of the Vaiśeṣika-sūtra is therefore significant:

**athāto dharmaṃ vyākhyāsyāmaḥ**  
“Now, therefore, we shall explain dharma.”

The next sūtra defines dharma as that from which arise both **abhyudaya** and **niḥśreyasa**:

**yato ’bhyudaya-niḥśreyasa-siddhiḥ sa dharmaḥ**  
“That from which the accomplishment of worldly flourishing and the supreme good arises is dharma.”

This shows the distinctive nature of Vaiśeṣika. It is a system of ontology, but its ontology is not divorced from ethics or mokṣa. To know reality correctly is not merely to classify things; it is to remove false identification, purify the mind, understand the distinction between self and not-self, and attain freedom from pain. Vaiśeṣika therefore operates within the broader puruṣārtha framework of dharma, artha, kāma, and mokṣa. Its study of matter, paramāṇu, qualities, motion, and causality is not reductionist. It studies the structure of the world within a framework directed toward dharma and mokṣa.

## Why the System Is Called Vaiśeṣika

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The name Vaiśeṣika is derived from **viśeṣa**, meaning particularity, distinction, differentiating feature, or specificity. Several explanations are offered in the tradition. The most philosophically important explanation is that Vaiśeṣika gives special attention to viśeṣa as the ultimate principle of differentiation, especially among eternal substances and paramāṇus.

If paramāṇus of the same class are partless, eternal, and similar in their general qualities, how is one paramāṇu distinct from another? Vaiśeṣika answers: by viśeṣa, the ultimate differentiator. Without viśeṣa, the plurality of reality could not be accounted for. Thus, Vaiśeṣika is the darśana that takes difference seriously. It does not dissolve the world into a single undifferentiated principle, nor does it treat plurality as mere appearance. It seeks to explain how unity, plurality, universality, and particularity coexist in reality.

## Textual Structure and Method

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Vaiśeṣika-sūtra is composed in aphoristic style. Like other sūtra texts, it is terse, compact, and meant to be unfolded through commentary. The tradition grew through major interpretive works such as Praśastapāda’s *Padārtha-dharma-saṅgraha*, Śaṅkara Miśra’s *Upaskāra*, and later expositions by Jayanārāyaṇa and Candrakānta.

The text is traditionally arranged into ten books or chapters, each divided into two āhnikas or sections. The broad architecture is as follows:

The first book introduces the padārthas, especially dravya, guṇa, and karma. The second examines the substances, especially the bhūtas such as earth, water, fire, air, and ether. The third discusses ātmā and manas, the self and the inner sense. The fourth deals with bodies and their constitution through paramāṇus. The fifth investigates action and motion. The sixth examines dharma and adharma. The seventh studies qualities and samavāya. The eighth and ninth discuss cognition, perception, inference, non-existence, and special forms of knowledge. The tenth examines the qualities of the self and causality.

The method of Vaiśeṣika is threefold:

1.  **uddeśa** — enumeration of the subject matter

2.  **lakṣaṇa** — definition through essential marks

3.  **parīkṣā** — examination, testing, and demonstration

This makes Vaiśeṣika a rigorous analytical system. It proceeds by identifying entities, defining them, distinguishing them from similar and dissimilar things, and examining their causal and relational functions.

## Padārtha: The Categories of Reality

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The central doctrine of Vaiśeṣika is the doctrine of **padārtha**. The term may be understood as “that which is denoted by a word,” “that which is knowable,” or “a category of reality.” In Vaiśeṣika, padārthas are not merely mental categories. They are real classes of entities that exist independently of our thought and become objects of knowledge because they are real.

The early Vaiśeṣika system enumerates six padārthas:

1.  **dravya** — substance

2.  **guṇa** — quality

3.  **karma** — action or motion

4.  **sāmānya** — universal or generality

5.  **viśeṣa** — particularity

6.  **samavāya** — inherence or inseparable relation

Later Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika adds a seventh:

7.  **abhāva** — non-existence or absence

Together, these categories form a comprehensive ontology. They are meant to include all that can be known, named, affirmed, denied, related, differentiated, and analysed.

## Dravya: The Nine Substances

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Dravya** is substance. It is the substratum in which qualities and actions reside. Without dravya, there can be no guṇa or karma. Vaiśeṣika therefore treats substance as the fundamental basis of empirical reality.

The nine dravyas are:

1.  **pṛthivī** — earth

2.  **āpas** — water

3.  **tejas** — fire

4.  **vāyu** — air

5.  **ākāśa** — ether

6.  **kāla** — time

7.  **dik** — space or direction

8.  **ātman** — self

9.  **manas** — mind or inner sense

The first four — earth, water, fire, and air — have paramāṇu forms. Their gross forms are non-eternal because they are produced by the combination of parts. Their ultimate paramāṇus are eternal, partless, and imperceptible.

**Pṛthivī** is associated with smell, taste, colour, and touch. **Āpas** is associated with taste, colour, touch, fluidity, and viscosity. **Tejas** is associated with colour and touch, especially heat and luminosity. **Vāyu** is associated with touch. **Ākāśa** is the substratum of sound. It is non-atomic, all-pervasive, and eternal. **Kāla** enables the cognition of before, after, simultaneity, priority, posteriority, quickness, and slowness. **Dik** enables the cognition of spatial location, direction, nearness, and remoteness. **Ātman** is the conscious self, the locus of cognition, pleasure, pain, desire, aversion, effort, dharma, adharma, and saṃskāra. **Manas** is the internal organ that mediates between the self and the senses.

## Guṇa: The Qualities

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Guṇa** is quality. A guṇa resides in a substance, does not itself possess qualities, and cannot independently cause conjunction or disjunction. Qualities make substances knowable and distinguishable.

The classical Vaiśeṣika list includes twenty-four guṇas:

1.  **rūpa** — colour

2.  **rasa** — taste

3.  **gandha** — smell

4.  **sparśa** — touch

5.  **saṃkhyā** — number

6.  **parimāṇa** — measure or magnitude

7.  **pṛthaktva** — separateness

8.  **saṃyoga** — conjunction

9.  **vibhāga** — disjunction

10. **paratva** — remoteness or priority

11. **aparatva** — nearness or posteriority

12. **buddhi** — cognition

13. **sukha** — pleasure

14. **duḥkha** — pain

15. **icchā** — desire

16. **dveṣa** — aversion

17. **prayatna** — effort

18. **gurutva** — heaviness or gravity

19. **dravatva** — fluidity

20. **sneha** — viscosity or cohesion

21. **saṃskāra** — impression, disposition, momentum, or latent force

22. **dharma** — puṇya (merit)

23. **adharma** — pāpa (demerit)

24. **śabda** — sound

Vaiśeṣika’s account of guṇa is important because it allows the system to explain both physical and psychological realities within one ontological framework. Colour, taste, smell, and touch explain perceptible material objects. Number, magnitude, separateness, conjunction, and disjunction explain structure and relation. Cognition, pleasure, pain, desire, aversion, and effort explain lived experience. Dharma and adharma connect action to moral consequence. Saṃskāra helps account for memory, momentum, and latent tendencies.

## Karma: Action and Motion

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Karma** in Vaiśeṣika primarily means action or motion, not ritual action in the Mīmāṃsā sense. It inheres in substance and is the independent cause of conjunction and disjunction.

Vaiśeṣika recognises five kinds of motion:

1.  **utkṣepaṇa** — upward movement

2.  **avakṣepaṇa** — downward movement

3.  **ākuñcana** — contraction

4.  **prasāraṇa** — expansion

5.  **gamana** — locomotion or movement from one place to another

Motion explains how substances come into contact, separate, combine, and produce effects. It is therefore central to Vaiśeṣika physics. The system discusses falling due to gurutva, upward movement due to impulse, motion caused by volition, motion caused by impact, movement of water through fluidity, evaporation through heat and air, and even the circulation of sap in plants through unseen causal forces.

## Sāmānya: Universal

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Sāmānya** is universality or generality. It is that by which many individuals are cognised as belonging to one class. For example, “treeness” is the sāmānya present in many trees; “substance-ness” is the sāmānya present in all substances.

Vaiśeṣika treats universals as real, not merely mental constructs. A universal is not identical with any one individual, yet it is present in many individuals. At the highest level stands **sattā** or existence, the most general universal, because it is common to substance, quality, and action.

## Viśeṣa: Particularity

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Viśeṣa** is the principle of ultimate differentiation. It explains how eternal entities such as paramāṇus, selves, space, time, ether, and mind remain distinct from one another. Without viśeṣa, all paramāṇus of earth would collapse into one indistinguishable entity, and all selves would become indistinguishable.

Viśeṣa is therefore the metaphysical ground of plurality. It is not merely visible difference; it is the irreducible particularity that makes each eternal substance itself and not another.

## Samavāya: Inherence

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Samavāya** is inseparable relation or inherence. It explains relations where two realities are distinct but cannot be separated without destroying the relevant entity. Examples include:

- the relation between whole and parts

- the relation between substance and quality

- the relation between action and the substance in which it occurs

- the relation between universal and individual

- the relation between eternal substances and their viśeṣas

Samavāya is essential because Vaiśeṣika does not reduce qualities to substances or universals to individuals. It must therefore explain how distinct ontological realities are intimately connected. Samavāya is that intimate connection.

## Abhāva: Non-Existence

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Though not included in the earliest sixfold enumeration, **abhāva** becomes an important seventh padārtha in later Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika. It means absence, non-being, or non-existence. The tradition recognises four forms:

1.  **prāgabhāva** — prior non-existence, as the absence of a pot before
    it is produced

2.  **pradhvaṃsābhāva** — posterior non-existence, as the absence of a
    pot after it is destroyed

3.  **atyantābhāva** — absolute non-existence, as the absence of a pot
    in a place where no pot ever exists

4.  **anyonyābhāva** — mutual non-existence, as the non-identity of a
    pot and a cloth

This doctrine shows the sharpness of Vaiśeṣika analysis. Reality includes not only positive entities but also absence, negation, non-relation, and difference.

## Vaiśeṣika Paramāṇuvāda

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Vaiśeṣika is famous for its doctrine of **Paramāṇuvāda**. The gross objects of the world are composite and therefore non-eternal. Their ultimate constituents are eternal, partless paramāṇus. Earth, water, fire, and air have paramāṇus. These paramāṇus combine to form dyads, triads, and larger aggregates, eventually producing perceptible objects.

This doctrine is closely related to **ārambha-vāda**, the view that the effect is a new beginning or fresh production. The effect is not merely a transformation of a pre-existing whole, as in some forms of pariṇāma-vāda, nor merely an appearance, as in vivarta-vāda. A pot is not already present in the clay in fully manifest form. It is newly produced through the conjunction and arrangement of causes.

Vaiśeṣika is therefore also associated with **asatkārya-vāda**, the view that the effect does not exist as an already formed entity before its production. Before production, the pot is absent; after production, it exists; after destruction, it is absent again. This allows Vaiśeṣika to give a robust account of creation, destruction, impermanence, and causal production.

## Ātman and Manas: The Self and the Inner Sense

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Vaiśeṣika is not a reductionist materialism because it accepts **ātman** as a real substance. The body, senses, and mind are not the ultimate seat of consciousness. Consciousness is an attribute of the self, arising under specific conditions.

The self is inferred through cognition, memory, pleasure, pain, desire, aversion, effort, merit, demerit, and the sense of “I.” The body cannot be the self because the body is material and perishable. The senses cannot be the self because they are instruments. Manas cannot be the self because it is paramāṇu-like in size, internal, and instrumental. The self is the enduring subject that owns experiences, remembers, desires, acts, and bears moral consequences.

**Manas** is the inner sense. It is paramāṇu-sized, eternal, and one in each embodied being. It connects the self with the senses. Without manas, even when sense organs and objects are present, cognition may not arise. This explains why we do not experience all sense objects simultaneously: manas attends to one sensory channel at a time. Cognition arises through the contact of self, mind, senses, and object.

## Knowledge, Pramāṇa, and the Nyāya Connection

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Vaiśeṣika is closely related to Nyāya. A common way of distinguishing them is to say that Vaiśeṣika is primarily ontological, while Nyāya is primarily epistemological and logical. Vaiśeṣika asks what exists; Nyāya asks how we know what exists. Over time, the two systems became deeply integrated as Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika.

Vaiśeṣika gives great importance to **pratyakṣa** and **anumāna** — perception and inference. Later discussions include verbal testimony, analogy, presumption, non-apprehension, and tradition, but these are often treated as reducible to perception or inference, or as forms dependent on inferential cognition. Nyāya, in contrast, classically accepts four pramāṇas: pratyakṣa, anumāna, upamāna, and śabda.

Vaiśeṣika’s epistemology is realistic. Truth is correspondence between cognition and reality. Things are knowable because they exist, and valid cognition reveals them as they are. This realism supports the entire padārtha system: substances, qualities, actions, universals, particularities, inherence, and absence are not merely subjective constructs but objective features of reality.

## Dharma, Adharma, and Adṛṣṭa

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Vaiśeṣika connects ontology to ethics through the doctrines of **dharma**, **adharma**, and **adṛṣṭa**. Dharma and adharma are qualities of the self. They are produced by action and become unseen causal forces that shape future experience.

**Adṛṣṭa** literally means “the unseen.” It is the latent moral-causal potency produced by past actions. It explains why beings are born into different bodies, circumstances, capacities, and experiences. It also plays a cosmological role: at the beginning of creation, the unseen force associated with selves contributes to the motion and combination of paramāṇus, thereby generating bodies and worlds suitable for experience and moral fruition.

Dharma is not merely social morality; it is that which supports both abhyudaya and niḥśreyasa. It purifies the mind, generates fitness for knowledge, and ultimately supports the rise of tattva-jñāna.

## Īśvara in Vaiśeṣika: A Historical Nuance

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The question of God in Vaiśeṣika requires nuance. Early Vaiśeṣika is often described as **nirīśvara** in the sense that Īśvara is not central to its earliest ontology in the way that paramāṇus, selves, karma, and adṛṣṭa are. However, later Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika commentators strongly introduce Īśvara as the supreme intelligent principle, the author of the Veda, and the regulator who directs adṛṣṭa and cosmic order.

Thus, Vaiśeṣika can be read in two layers: as an early realist ontology centred on padārtha, paramāṇu, and adṛṣṭa, and as a later Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika system in which Īśvara plays an explicit role in creation, Vedic authority, and moral governance.

## Nature, Science, and the Study of the Physical World

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Vaiśeṣika contains striking discussions that resemble natural philosophy. It investigates:

- paramāṇu-combination

- heat and transformation

- sound and its substratum

- gurutva and falling

- fluidity and flow

- impulse and momentum

- conjunction and disjunction

- bodily formation

- sense organs and their objects

- plant life and circulation

- dream, memory, cognition, and error

Its purpose is not modern science in the contemporary laboratory sense, but it does show a rigorous concern for the structure of the physical world. It analyses the material universe without denying the reality of self, morality, liberation, and transcendence. In this sense, Vaiśeṣika is a rare synthesis of ontological realism, natural inquiry, ethical causality, and spiritual purpose.

## Mokṣa: Liberation as the Cessation of Pain

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For Vaiśeṣika, mokṣa or apavarga is the supreme good. It is described primarily as the complete cessation of duḥkha. This differs from systems that describe liberation as eternal bliss or union with Brahman. Vaiśeṣika is cautious: pleasure and pain are both qualities of the embodied self under conditions of contact, karma, and mental activity. Liberation is the state in which the causes of suffering are neutralised and the self is no longer bound to embodiment, desire, aversion, merit, demerit, and karmic experience.

The path to mokṣa is through tattva-jñāna: knowledge of reality as it is. By understanding the distinction between self and not-self, by grasping the true nature of padārthas, by purifying the mind through dharma, and by exhausting adṛṣṭa, the self becomes free from the cycle of birth and suffering.

## The Significance of Vaiśeṣika

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Vaiśeṣika Darśana is one of the most sophisticated ontological systems in world philosophy. Its importance lies in several contributions.

First, it gives a detailed category-system for analysing reality. Second, it develops a robust Paramāṇuvāda without reducing consciousness to matter. Third, it treats universals, particulars, inherence, absence, and causality with remarkable precision. Fourth, it integrates metaphysics with ethics through dharma, adharma, and adṛṣṭa. Fifth, it links knowledge to liberation, making ontology a spiritual discipline.

Vaiśeṣika teaches that liberation does not come from vague belief or sentiment, but from precise knowledge. To know the world rightly is to know the not-self rightly; to know the not-self rightly is to distinguish it from the self; and to distinguish the self from the not-self is to remove the fundamental confusion that sustains bondage. Thus, the journey of Vaiśeṣika begins with the smallest paramāṇu and ends with the freedom of the self.

In its full landscape, Vaiśeṣika is not merely “Indian Paramāṇuvāda.” It is a darśana of reality, difference, causality, embodiment, knowledge, dharma, and liberation. It stands as a powerful example of how Indian thought can analyse the concrete world in all its detail while never losing sight of the highest human aim: niḥśreyasa, the supreme good.

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)
